sections.content: An Emergency Position Indicating Radio Beacon (EPIRB) is a distress beacon that transmits on 406 MHz to the Cospas-Sarsat satellite system. When activated, it sends a coded signal containing the vessel's identity and, if GPS-equipped, its precise position. A secondary signal on 121.5 MHz serves as a homing frequency for SAR aircraft.

sections.title: Satellite Detection
sections.content: A 406 MHz EPIRB transmits your vessel's identity and position to Cospas-Sarsat satellites. Detection time is typically under an hour with MEOSAR, and position accuracy is within 5 km (or 100 m with internal GPS).

EPIRBs must be registered with the national authority and the registration details kept current. Float-free EPIRBs activate automatically when the hydrostatic release mechanism detects submersion. Battery life is a minimum of 48 hours once activated.

sections.prompt: On which frequencies does a 406 MHz EPIRB transmit?
sections.explanation: A 406 MHz EPIRB transmits a digital identity signal on 406 MHz (detected by Cospas-Sarsat satellites) and a homing signal on 121.5 MHz for SAR aircraft to locate the beacon.
